%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 4.3-3 Stability

> **Section Objective:** Learn how transfer-function poles determine external stability, and when they do not reveal internal stability.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- BIBO stability from poles
- proper vs improper transfer functions
- asymptotic and marginal stability
- pole-zero cancellation trap


## 1. BIBO Stability from Transfer-Function Poles

\(H(s)\) and its inverse Laplace transform \(h(t)\) are **external** descriptions — they capture only what is measurable from the input-output terminals of the system.

**What are poles?** Poles are values of \(s\) where \(H(s)\) becomes infinite. A pole at \(s = \sigma + j\omega\) contributes a term proportional to \(e^{\sigma t}\cos(\omega t)\) in \(h(t)\).

**Why LHP matters:** LHP means \(\sigma < 0\), so the real part is negative. A negative real part forces \(e^{\sigma t} \to 0\) as \(t \to \infty\), making \(h(t)\) absolutely integrable — the definition of BIBO stability.

### EXAM TRIGGER

When a problem asks for BIBO stability and gives \(H(s)\), **cancel common factors first**, then locate the poles of the reduced expression.

### COMMON MISUSE

Using the uncancelled denominator automatically — even when a common factor in numerator and denominator would cancel — can lead to counting a pole that does not actually appear in the reduced \(H(s)\).

$$\text{BIBO stable} \Longleftrightarrow \text{all poles of } H(s) \text{ are in the LHP}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Improper Transfer Functions Are BIBO-Unstable

When the numerator degree \(M\) exceeds the denominator degree \(N\), polynomial long division is required. The result splits \(H(s)\) into a **polynomial part** \(R(s)\) and a **proper remainder** \(\widetilde{H}(s)\).

**Why this is dangerous:** The polynomial part \(R(s)\) corresponds to differentiator-like behavior in the time domain. A differentiator can turn a bounded step input into an impulse — an unbounded output — so the system fails the BIBO test regardless of where the finite poles sit.

**Textbook example:** For \(H(s) = \dfrac{s^3 + 4s^2 + 4s + 5}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\), long division gives

$$H(s) = s + \frac{s^2 + 2s + 5}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$$

The leading \(s\) term is the polynomial part \(R(s)\), which is a pure differentiator.

### EXAM TRIGGER

If \(M > N\), declare **BIBO-unstable immediately** — no full pole test needed.

### COMMON MISUSE

Checking only pole locations while ignoring improperness. The finite poles of \(\widetilde{H}(s)\) may all be in the LHP, but the polynomial part still makes the system BIBO-unstable.

$$H(s) = R(s) + \widetilde{H}(s) \quad \text{when } M > N$$

## 3. Asymptotic Stability Needs the No-Cancellation Condition

\(H(s) = P(s)/Q(s)\) is an **external** description. The system's **characteristic polynomial** is internal and governs all internal modes. When \(P(s)\) and \(Q(s)\) share no common factors, no pole-zero cancellation has occurred, so the poles of \(H(s)\) are exactly the characteristic roots. In that case, pole locations fully determine internal (asymptotic) stability.

**Three compact rules (assuming no cancellation):**

- **Asymptotically stable:** all poles are strictly in the LHP (negative real parts).
- **Unstable:** at least one pole is in the RHP, **or** there are repeated poles on the imaginary axis.
- **Marginally stable:** no RHP poles, and there are some **unrepeated** poles on the imaginary axis.

#### Warning

The **locations of zeros** of \(H(s)\) play no role in determining stability under these criteria. Only pole locations matter.

$$P(s) \text{ and } Q(s) \text{ have no common factors} \Longrightarrow \text{poles of } H(s) \text{ reveal internal stability}$$

## 4. Pole-Zero Cancellation Can Hide Instability

Consider a cascade of two subsystems: \(H_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s-1}\) and \(H_2(s) = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1}\).

The external product reduces to \(H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}\), with a single visible pole at \(s = -1\). From the outside, this looks BIBO-stable.

**The hidden danger:** The pole at \(s = 1\) — which is in the RHP — came from the internal subsystem \(H_1(s)\). It canceled algebraically with the zero of \(H_2(s)\), so it disappears from the external transfer function. But the internal mode associated with \(s = 1\) can still exist and grow inside the system.

### EXAM TRAP

Cancellation can make the black-box transfer function appear BIBO-stable while **internal (asymptotic) stability is not guaranteed**. Always ask whether any cancellation has occurred before claiming full stability.

$$H(s) = H_1(s)\,H_2(s) = \frac{1}{s-1} \cdot \frac{s-1}{s+1} = \frac{1}{s+1}$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **BIBO stable** \(\Longleftrightarrow\) all poles of \(H(s)\) are in the LHP (after cancellation); one RHP pole is enough to fail.
- **Improper** \(H(s) = R(s) + \widetilde{H}(s)\) when \(M > N\): the polynomial part \(R(s)\) causes BIBO failure — declare unstable immediately.
- **No common factors** in \(P(s)\) and \(Q(s)\) \(\Rightarrow\) poles reveal internal stability: all-LHP = asymptotic; unrepeated imaginary-axis = marginal; RHP or repeated imaginary-axis = unstable.
- **Cascade cancellation** \(H(s) = H_1(s)H_2(s)\) can hide an internal unstable pole — the reduced \(H(s)\) may look stable while a canceled RHP pole persists internally.

*Next, use these stability ideas when interpreting inverse systems and transformed network models.*
